The best low-friction boundary looks like a model that owns handlers and polling while the template keeps raw `bind:` fields. I’m checking `RuntimeQuickMenu` because it already acts like a consumer of a view-model slice.

Priority order, highest value first:

1. Extract `SettingsModel.runtime.draft` first. The biggest state cluster starts at [SettingsPage.svelte:81](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L81), and it already has clean hydrate/serialize/diff boundaries at [SettingsPage.svelte:957](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L957), [SettingsPage.svelte:1041](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1041), [SettingsPage.svelte:1108](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1108), [SettingsPage.svelte:1195](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1195), [SettingsPage.svelte:1242](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1242), and [SettingsPage.svelte:1266](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1266). Template churn stays low if the model keeps the same draft field names used by direct `bind:` sites like [SettingsPage.svelte:1811](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1811), [SettingsPage.svelte:1830](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1830), [SettingsPage.svelte:1983](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1983), [SettingsPage.svelte:2201](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L2201), and [SettingsPage.svelte:2486](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L2486).

2. Extract a `runtime.jobs` or `runtime.status` controller next. The direct API/timer side effects are already concentrated in [SettingsPage.svelte:1028](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1028), [SettingsPage.svelte:1294](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1294), [SettingsPage.svelte:1317](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1317), [SettingsPage.svelte:1333](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1333), [SettingsPage.svelte:1345](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1345), [SettingsPage.svelte:1363](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1363), and the polling effect at [SettingsPage.svelte:1460](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1460). This can move with almost no markup change because the template only calls handlers at [SettingsPage.svelte:2092](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L2092), [SettingsPage.svelte:2101](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L2101), and [SettingsPage.svelte:2399](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L2399) and otherwise reads flags/status objects.

3. Extract `SettingsModel.account` as a separate small model. Auth/settings state is at [SettingsPage.svelte:66](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L66), credential list state at [SettingsPage.svelte:74](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L74), and credential form draft at [SettingsPage.svelte:817](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L817). The imperative side effects are already isolated in [SettingsPage.svelte:900](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L900), [SettingsPage.svelte:939](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L939), [SettingsPage.svelte:1393](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1393), [SettingsPage.svelte:1407](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1407), [SettingsPage.svelte:1417](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1417), and [SettingsPage.svelte:1441](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1441). This covers the clipboard side effect too, and the template impact is mostly swapping handler refs at [SettingsPage.svelte:1605](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1605), [SettingsPage.svelte:1623](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1623), [SettingsPage.svelte:1695](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1695), and [SettingsPage.svelte:1725](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1725).

4. Move section navigation into a tiny helper, not necessarily the core model. DOM access and navigation side effects are isolated at [SettingsPage.svelte:58](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L58), [SettingsPage.svelte:446](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L446), [SettingsPage.svelte:452](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L452), and [SettingsPage.svelte:471](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L471). This is low-risk because the template only consumes it via [SettingsPage.svelte:1535](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1535) and [SettingsPage.svelte:1560](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L1560).

5. Use the existing quick-menu boundary as the first model-facing DTO. The prop bag passed into `RuntimeQuickMenu` at [SettingsPage.svelte:2590](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L2590) through [SettingsPage.svelte:2631](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L2631) is already a view-model contract, and the local toggle/update façade is at [SettingsPage.svelte:476](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L476). That makes `settings-page/settings-model.ts` a clean addition beside the existing presentation types in [models.ts:1](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/settings-page/models.ts#L1).

6. Defer pure formatter extraction. Helpers like [SettingsPage.svelte:215](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L215), [SettingsPage.svelte:372](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L372), [SettingsPage.svelte:776](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L776), [SettingsPage.svelte:823](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L823), and [SettingsPage.svelte:830](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L830) are easy to move later, but they do not reduce the main coupling nearly as much as the side-effectful runtime/account handlers.

DOM access and navigation side effects are isolated at [SettingsPage.svelte:58](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L58), [SettingsPage.svelte:446](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L446), [SettingsPage.svelte:452](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L452), and [SettingsPage.svelte:471](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L471). 5. Use the existing quick-menu boundary as the first model-facing DTO. The prop bag passed into `RuntimeQuickMenu` at [SettingsPage.svelte:2590](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L2590) through [SettingsPage.svelte:2631](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L2631) is already a view-model contract, and the local toggle/update façade is at [SettingsPage.svelte:476](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L476). That makes `settings-page/settings-model.ts` a clean addition beside the existing presentation types in [models.ts:1](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/settings-page/models.ts#L1).

6. Defer pure formatter extraction. Helpers like [SettingsPage.svelte:215](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L215), [SettingsPage.svelte:372](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L372), [SettingsPage.svelte:776](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L776), [SettingsPage.svelte:823](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L823), and [SettingsPage.svelte:830](~/opensession/packages/ui/src/components/SettingsPage.svelte#L830) are easy to move later, but they do not reduce the main coupling nearly as much as the side-effectful runtime/account handlers.
